title: Psalms 58
subtitle: Do You Rulers Indeed Speak Justly?

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Even from Birth the Wicked Go Astray”

heb.paragraph: The opening word אֵלֶם (ʾēlem) is notoriously difficult. It could mean 'silence' (are you silent when you should speak justice?) or be repointed as אֵלִם (ʾēlim, 'gods/rulers'). The LXX reads 'indeed' (ἄρα). Most modern translations follow the ancient versions in reading it as an address to corrupt judges or divine beings who fail their charge. The ambiguity itself may be intentional — those who should speak justice are mute.

"Your hands mete out violence (חָמָס) on the earth" (v.2). The noun חָמָס denotes violent wrongdoing, oppression that shatters social order. It's the word God uses to describe the pre-flood world (Gen 6:11). The rulers' hands — instruments meant for justice — instead dispense destruction. The accusation is total: heart devises, hands distribute.

"Their venom (חֲמַת) is like the venom of a snake" (v.4). The wicked are venomous from birth — not made evil by circumstance but inherently poisonous. The snake imagery intensifies: they are like a cobra (פֶּתֶן) that stops its ears against the charmer. They are deliberately deaf to wisdom, immune to persuasion. Evil that cannot be reasoned with.

"The charmer (מְחַבֵּר), however skillful the enchanter may be" (v.5). The חֹבֵר (ḥōbēr) was a snake-charmer who could render serpents harmless through incantation. The psalmist's point: these wicked rulers are worse than snakes — they've made themselves immune to any voice that might restrain them. Not even the most skilled moral appeal can penetrate their deafness.

notes.note: "Do you rulers speak justly? No, in your heart you devise injustice." Corrupt leadership corrupts everything beneath it.
"Even from birth the wicked go astray. Their venom is like the venom of a snake, like a deaf cobra." The deaf-cobra image: immune to reason, resistant to charm. Some evil cannot be talked down.

refs.note: Paul quotes 'the poison of vipers is on their lips' from Psalm 140:3 in his indictment of universal human sinfulness.
The tongue as a restless evil full of deadly poison develops the psalm's serpent imagery.
Jesus calling Pharisees a brood of vipers uses the same serpentine language for corrupt speech.

hist.context: Psalm 58 is an imprecatory psalm addressing corrupt judges ('gods' or 'mighty ones' in v.1 may refer to divine council members or human rulers). The deaf cobra image (vv.4-5) draws on ancient Near Eastern snake-charming traditions. The accusation that the wicked are 'astray from birth' parallels the 'original sin' concept without using that later terminology.

sections.header: Verses 6–11 — “The Righteous Will Be Glad When They Are Avenged”

heb.paragraph: "Break (הֲרָס) the teeth in their mouths, O God" (v.6). The imperative הֲרָס means to demolish, tear down, destroy. The psalmist asks God to disarm the predators by removing their weapons. Teeth and fangs represent the capacity to devour the vulnerable. The prayer is brutal but proportional — those who bite should lose the means to bite.

"Let them vanish (יִמָּאֲסוּ) like water that flows away" (v.7). The verb מָאַס in this form means to dissolve, flow away, cease to exist. The psalmist heaps up images of dissolution: water absorbed by ground, arrows that shatter, a slug leaving a slime trail as it melts, a stillborn that never sees sun. Evil should un-become.

"May they be like a slug (שַׁבְּלוּל) that melts away as it moves" (v.8). The שַׁבְּלוּל is a snail or slug — a creature that seems to dissolve into its own slime trail. The ancients observed that slugs leave a glistening path and shrink as they move, as if consuming themselves. The wicked should similarly self-destruct, leaving only a residue of their former menace.

"When they dip (יִרְחַץ) their feet in the blood of the wicked" (v.10). The verb רָחַץ means to wash, bathe. This disturbing image — the righteous washing feet in blood — echoes ancient Near Eastern victory celebrations. It's not sadism but vindication: the blood of oppressors becomes evidence that God judges. The righteous don't create the blood; they witness that justice finally arrived.

notes.note: "Break the teeth of the wicked." The imprecation: David asks God to disarm the predators. Teeth are the lion’s weapon; breaking them renders the attacker harmless.

"Surely there is a reward for the righteous; surely there is a God who judges the earth." The psalm’s conclusion: justice exists because the Judge exists. The "surely" is emphatic — asserting what the corrupt judges deny.

refs.note: The multitude praising God for true and just judgments fulfills the psalm's vision of vindication.
God paying back trouble to those who trouble believers echoes the psalm's retributive justice.
Martyrs crying 'how long?' until judgment comes reflects the same longing for divine vindication.

hist.context: The violent imagery of broken teeth, vanishing like water, and trampling in blood (vv.6-10) reflects ancient Near Eastern curse formulas and warfare language. The righteous 'bathing feet in the blood of the wicked' shocks modern readers but expresses the psalmist's confidence that evil will not triumph indefinitely. The psalm insists that a moral order governs reality.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Accuser of corrupt rulers who pervert justice (vv.1-2,6-11)
ppl.text: David addresses 'rulers' (or possibly 'gods' — the Hebrew elim is ambiguous) who 'devise injustice' and whose 'hands mete out violence on the earth' (vv.1-2). His imprecations are vivid: 'Break the teeth in their mouths, O God; tear out the fangs of those lions' (v.6). The wicked are compared to a cobra that 'stops its ears' against the charmer (vv.4-5) — they are deliberately deaf to justice. This is among the 'hard psalms' that challenge modern readers, but in context it is a cry for God to enforce the justice that human courts have abandoned.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The righteous judge who will vindicate (vv.6,11)
ppl.text: David calls on God to act where human justice has failed. The psalm's confidence rests on a final reckoning: 'Surely the righteous still are rewarded; surely there is a God who judges the earth' (v.11). This is not wishful thinking but a theological axiom — if God exists, injustice cannot be the last word. The violent imagery (breaking teeth, like a 'slug melting away,' like a 'stillborn child,' vv.6-8) reflects the psalmist's belief that evil is inherently self-destructive; he asks God to accelerate what sin's own nature guarantees.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVDo you rulers indeed speak justly? Do you judge people with equity?ESVDo you indeed decree what is right, you gods? Do you judge the children of man uprightly?NIVBefore your pots can feel the heat of the thorns— whether they be green or dry—the wicked will be swept away.ESVSooner than your pots can feel the heat of thorns, whether green or ablaze, may he sweep them away!

rec.text: ""Do you rulers speak justly? No, in your heart you devise injustice." Corrupt leadership corrupts everything beneath it."

rows.label: Corrupt Judges — 58:1–5
rows.text: "Do you rulers speak justly?" Deaf cobras. Injustice from birth.

hebtext.note: "Do you rulers indeed speak justly (ṣedeq)? Do you judge people with equity?" (v.1). An indictment of corrupt judges. Their hearts devise injustice, their hands mete out violence (v.2). The wicked are like venomous snakes, deaf to the charmer’s tune (vv.3–5). They are corrupt FROM BIRTH — innate wickedness, not learned behaviour.

"Surely there is a reward (pərî) for the righteous; surely there is a God who judges the earth" (v.11). The imprecatory section: break their teeth (v.6), let them vanish like water (v.7), like a slug that melts (v.8), like a stillborn that never sees the sun (v.8). The violence of the imagery matches the violence of the injustice. The psalm’s final declaration: there IS a God who judges.

themes.note: Justice peaks at 10: corrupt judges indicted + "God who judges the earth." Judgment: imprecatory imagery matches the injustice. The harshest psalm so far.
